source.title: A dual operator view of habitual behavior reflecting cortical and striatal dynamics.

confidence_rationale: Strong empirical paper combining single-unit recording in rat sensorimotor striatum and infralimbic cortex with selective optogenetic disruption (Neuron 2013, Graybiel lab). The headline finding is that habit crystallization requires *both* a striatal action-bracketing pattern (start-spike + stop-spike framing the routine, insensitive to reward devaluation) and a separately-developing infralimbic ensemble that closely tracks habit state, with optogenetic disruption of infralimbic activity preventing habit formation. For SD-034 closure operator, the start/stop-bracketing pattern is the closest direct neural analogue of the 'detect committed sequence completion' function, complementing Barnes 2011 (advance-cued action-boundary patterns) by showing that the same bracketing structure spans purposeful->habitual transitions. Held below 0.85 because (a) bracketing is detected at action-routine boundaries rather than at rule_state satisfaction specifically, and (b) the OFC/ACC-located closure SD-034 commits to is not the structure recorded here.
failure_signatures: The dual operator framework localises completion-detection to sensorimotor striatum + infralimbic cortex, not OFC/ACC. SD-034 names OFC/ACC sequence-completion cells as the substrate (Schuck 2016 / Rich & Shapiro 2009 in the existing entries). If the closure operator is anatomically distributed across striatum + IL + OFC/ACC, SD-034 may need to elevate from a single-substrate commitment to a multi-region operator -- and the V3 implementation would then need to decide which substrate carries the canonical 'done' token.

The bracketing pattern in Smith & Graybiel 2013 is for *habitual* routines (overtrained, devaluation-insensitive). SD-034's closure operator is meant to fire on rule_state satisfaction in flexible / planned mode as well -- not just for crystallised habits. If the bracketing mechanism is specific to the habitual mode, the support generalises only to one of the operating modes SD-034 needs to handle, and the planned-mode closure mechanism remains unspecified.

Optogenetic disruption of IL activity prevents habit *formation*, not necessarily habit *closure / release*. The dual operator paper does not directly test what happens when an infralimbic ensemble that has crystallised around a habit needs to release commitment on satisfaction. The closure-as-release function is one ARC removed from what was tested.

mapping.source_claim_statement: As rats overtrain on maze tasks, behaviour shifts from purposeful to habitual. This shift coincides with two simultaneously-engaged but separately-developing neural ensembles: a fast-acquired action-bracketing pattern in sensorimotor striatum (start-spike at routine onset, stop-spike at routine completion, devaluation-insensitive but automaticity-sensitive) and a late-developing pattern in upper layers of infralimbic neocortex that closely tracks habit state. Selective optogenetic disruption of infralimbic activity during overtraining prevents habit formation, supporting a dual-operator view in which both regions are necessary for habit crystallization.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-034 specifies a closure operator that detects committed rule_state satisfaction and emits release + No-Go bias. Smith & Graybiel 2013 supports the closure-detection component by demonstrating start/stop bracketing patterns at action-routine boundaries: the stop-spike at routine completion is the closest direct neural analogue of a 'done' token. The dual-operator finding (striatum + IL both required) is also informative for SD-034's substrate commitment: the closure operation may be anatomically distributed across multiple regions rather than localised to OFC/ACC alone. The paper therefore (a) supports the existence of an empirically-grounded completion-detection mechanism that could ground SD-034 implementation, and (b) flags that the SD-034 architectural commitment to OFC/ACC may be too narrow.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Smith & Graybiel 2013 records during habitual maze-running, not during rule-conditional decision-making. The bracketing pattern is specific to crystallised habit routines. SD-034's closure operator is meant to fire on rule_state satisfaction in flexible / planned operating modes too, where the same bracketing mechanism may or may not apply. Generalising habit-bracketing to closure-on-rule-satisfaction is a one-step transfer that the paper does not strictly support but does not contradict either. Anatomical localisation is also distinct: striatum + infralimbic cortex are separately-recorded substrates, and SD-034's existing OFC/ACC commitment is not directly addressed.
mapping.source_context: Rat sensorimotor striatum and infralimbic cortex single-unit recordings during overtrained T-maze tasks; longitudinal acquisition spanning purposeful->habitual transition; selective optogenetic disruption of IL activity in transgenic rats. Two recording sites, three groups (control, IL-disrupted-during-acquisition, IL-disrupted-after-acquisition).

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Neuron 2013, Graybiel lab, dual-region single-unit + causal optogenetic manipulation, well-cited as the canonical dual-operator paper for habit). Mapping fidelity moderate-good: action-bracketing maps onto closure-detection but only for the habit-mode case; multi-region distribution of the closure mechanism suggests SD-034 may need to elevate from single-substrate to operator-distributed-across-regions. Transfer risk moderate: habit-mode-only support, plus the non-canonical OFC/ACC localisation in this paper compared to SD-034's stated substrate.
